title: How the sensation of being hungry triggers childhood neglect patterns
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
The knot in my stomach that isn't hunger

I know that feeling. The empty ache. The hollow pit that says something is missing. And I know how fast my brain translates that into something much older, much darker. It says I'm not getting what I need. It says no one is coming. It says I'm alone with this gnawing sensation, and it's my fault for needing anything at all.

That's the thing about hunger for those of us who grew up with neglect. It's never just hunger. It's a time machine that drops you right back into a childhood where your basic needs were met inconsistently, or not at all. Your nervous system doesn't distinguish between "I haven't eaten in six hours" and "I was left alone in my crib crying for food." To your body, both register as the same threat.

Here's the thing. Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed Polyvagal Theory, would call this neuroception. Your body scanning for safety or danger below the level of conscious thought. And when that hunger signal hits, your nervous system might interpret it as danger. Not because food is scarce. But because scarcity was your first language.

I've watched myself spiral. A slightly empty stomach, and suddenly I'm irritable. Snapping at people. Feeling abandoned. Feeling like no one cares if I eat or starve. And I know, intellectually, that I have a fridge full of food. But the feeling doesn't care about the fridge. The feeling lives in a body that learned, long ago, that hunger meant nobody was coming to help.

Think about it. If you were neglected as a child, your needs weren't just delayed. They were ignored. Dismissed. Treated as inconvenient. So your body learned a terrible lesson: needing things is dangerous. Needing things gets you hurt. And hunger is the most basic need there is. So when it shows up, your whole system might brace for impact.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker, the author, talks extensively about how emotional flashbacks can be triggered by bodily sensations. Hunger is one of those sensations. It's not just about food. It's about the feeling of being unseen, unheard, unmet.

The freeze response and the forgotten meal

I used to wonder why I would forget to eat for hours. Why I could be starving but somehow not notice until I was shaking. It wasn't discipline. It wasn't intermittent fasting. It was dissociation. A classic freeze response. When your body learned that noticing hunger was too painful, you learn to ignore it. To push past it. To stay busy so you don't have to feel the emptiness.

Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing, would say that trauma lives in the body. And the body's signals get scrambled. Hunger, thirst, fatigue, they all get mixed up with shame and fear and abandonment. So you might not even recognize you're hungry. You just feel wrong. Anxious. Angry. Sad. And you have no idea why.

I've done this dance a thousand times. I'm working, focused, productive. Then suddenly I'm shaky and irritable and I can't think straight. And I realize I haven't eaten since yesterday. My body was sending signals, but I had trained myself to ignore them. Because listening to my body meant listening to a child who was told their needs didn't matter.

Wild, right? That the very act of feeding yourself can feel like a betrayal of an old survival strategy. The strategy that said "don't need anything, don't ask for anything, don't feel anything." And eating means you're admitting you have needs. That you're human. That you're vulnerable. That can feel terrifying.

If you are curious about inherited trauma patterns, you might find value in It Didn't Start with You (paid link). Mark Wolynn's work explores how family trauma can shape our responses to basic bodily needs. It's not just your personal history. It might be your mother's hunger. Your grandmother's scarcity. Passed down in ways you never expected.

The shame of needing to eat

There's a specific shame that comes with hunger for neglect survivors. It's not just "I'm hungry." It's "I'm hungry again." Like you're failing. Like you're too needy. Like you should be able to go longer, do more, require less. This is the voice of neglect. The voice that said your needs were an inconvenience. A burden. Something to be minimized.

I've had full conversations with myself about whether I should eat. "You just ate four hours ago. You shouldn't be hungry. You're being dramatic. Just wait. Drink water. Push through it." And this isn't discipline. This is reenacting neglect. I am doing to myself what was done to me. Ignoring my own hunger. Dismissing my own need. Treating myself like I didn't matter.

Janina Fisher, who wrote about structural dissociation, would call this an internal conflict between parts. One part of you is a hungry child who needs food and comfort. Another part is the critical voice that says you don't deserve it. Another part is the freeze response that checks out entirely. And you're stuck in the middle, not sure who to listen to.

Here's what I've started to notice. When I feel that hunger shame, I pause. I put my hand on my stomach. I say out loud, "I'm hungry. That's okay. I'm allowed to eat." It sounds ridiculous. It sounds too simple. But saying it out loud interrupts the old pattern. It tells my nervous system that someone is paying attention. That someone cares. That someone is coming to help.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link). It has exercises specifically for identifying these body-based triggers and responding to them differently. Not with shame. With curiosity. With care.

Feeding yourself as a radical act

I want to be honest with you. Learning to eat regularly has been one of the hardest parts of my recovery. Not because I don't know how to cook. Not because I don't have food. But because feeding myself means I have to admit I'm worth feeding. And that is a deeply uncomfortable realization for someone who was taught otherwise.

Every meal is a choice. Every snack is a decision. And each one is an opportunity to either repeat the old pattern or break it. You can skip the meal and prove to yourself that you don't need anything. Or you can eat and prove to yourself that you matter. That your needs are valid. That you deserve to be nourished.

I'm not saying this is easy. Some days I still forget to eat. Some days I still feel that old shame. But I'm getting better at noticing it. At recognizing that hunger is not abandonment. Hunger is just hunger. A signal from my body that it needs fuel. Nothing more, nothing less.

Dan Siegel talks about integration. The ability to link different parts of our experience together. When you can feel hungry and not fall into the neglect pattern, you are integrating. You are telling your brain that the past is over. That you are safe now. That you can have needs and they will be met.

I've also found that weighted blankets help calm the nervous system when these old patterns get activated. A weighted blanket that many find calming for their nervous system is YnM Weighted Blanket 15 lbs (paid link). It provides a sense of containment that can help you stay present when hunger triggers feelings of falling apart.

What to do when hunger triggers you

First, notice. Just notice the feeling in your body. The emptiness. The ache. The tightness. Don't try to change it. Don't judge it. Just observe it. Say to yourself, "I notice hunger. And I notice that my body is reacting as if this is dangerous." That simple act of noticing can shift you out of the freeze and into presence.

Second, breathe. Take three slow breaths. In through your nose. Out through your mouth. This activates your ventral vagal system, the part of your nervous system that supports connection and calm. It tells your body that even though you're hungry, you're not in danger. You're just in need of food.

Third, eat. Even if it's just a cracker. Even if it's just a piece of fruit. The act of eating is a message to your nervous system. It says, "I am here. I am taking care of you. You are not alone." And every time you do this, you weaken the old pattern. You build a new one.

Fourth, be gentle with yourself. If you skipped meals today, don't shame yourself. Don't add guilt on top of hunger. Just notice. And try again tomorrow. Recovery is not about perfection. It's about practice. yourself, one meal at a time.

Sit with that. Your hunger is not a failure. It's a signal. And you get to decide what that signal means. You get to decide if it means "I'm in danger" or "I need to eat." And every time you choose the second one, you are healing. You are telling your inner child that they are seen. That they are safe. That they are worth feeding.

  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link)
